description.s: Hobson draws from several foundational ideas in interactive computing, personal information management, and live programming environments. Understanding these influences helps explain why the system works the way it does.
describes twelve attributes that make interactive applications feel natural and empowering. Hobson aspires to embody all of them.
Saving an item re-renders it immediately
Items are granular; edits are atomic
Links and context menus reveal pathways
The tool disappears; you touch the material
Everything is an item; no hidden layers
Edit code items while system runs
Actions are reversible, system doesn't break
Safe mode, type validation, error capture
Source and runtime are the same
Hobson can render and edit itself
See data flow in real time

pioneered the idea that the entire development environment--objects, classes, methods, and state--lives in a persistent "image" rather than external files. When you close Smalltalk, you save the image; when you reopen it, everything is exactly as you left it.
: All items persist in the browser's IndexedDB
: Code items are evaluated directly from storage
: Close the tab, reopen it, and your workspace remains
: Edit a kernel module, reload, and changes take effect
Unlike Smalltalk, Hobson stores code as text (ES modules) rather than bytecode, enabling inspection and editing with standard tools.

) cultures emphasize the REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) as the primary interface for development. You write expressions, evaluate them immediately, see results, and iterate. The distinction between "writing code" and "running code" dissolves.
Provides full access to the kernel API
Allows creating, querying, and modifying items directly
Enables testing code before persisting it as a code item
The system can be operated entirely through the REPL if desired--the graphical interface is a convenience, not a requirement.
explore what he calls an "itemized" approach to personal computing. Key ideas:
Any item can be moved anywhere in the system. There are no application silos--a note can become a child of a project, a search result, or a workspace item.
Any item can contain references to any other items. This enables:
Bidirectional linking (via tags or explicit references)
Data storage is independent of presentation. The same item can be rendered as:
Views are items too--users can modify or create them.
Rather than folders and files, everything forms a knowledge graph. Items connect through:
popularized bidirectional linking in note-taking. Hobson adapts this with
Clicking opens the linked item (as sibling window in spatial view, or navigation otherwise).
Embeds the entire item inline, rendered with its own view.
Embeds specific fields, named regions, or line ranges.
const modules = await api.query({ type: api.IDS.KERNEL_MODULE }); return modules.map(m =>
Dynamic content generated at render time.
Hobson synthesizes these influences into a coherent system:
Persistence model: everything in the image
Interaction model: REPL-first, code as data
Data model: items, types, universal containers
The result is a system where:
You can inspect and modify anything
Navigation and structure emerge from usage
The tool and the content are made of the same material
